title: Breach Discovery — Time-to-Detect from First PHI Indicator

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

spl:
`notable` HIPAA_Breach_Candidate=1 earliest=-90d
| eval detect_time=_time
| join IncidentId type=left [
    search index=dlp sourcetype="o365:dlp" earliest=-90d
    | eval first_indicator=strptime(CreatedTime,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(first_indicator) as first_dlp by IncidentId ]
| eval hours_to_detect=round((detect_time-first_dlp)/3600,2)
| table IncidentId, first_dlp, detect_time, hours_to_detect, rule_name, status
| sort - hours_to_detect
description: Measures elapsed time between earliest technical indicator of a potential PHI incident and formal incident creation so discovery timelines support Breach Notification Rule evidence.
value: Measures elapsed time between earliest technical indicator of a potential PHI incident and formal incident creation so discovery timelines support Breach Notification Rule evidence.
grandmaExplanation: Measures elapsed time between earliest technical indicator of a potential health data incident and formal incident creation so discovery timelines support Breach Notification Rule evidence — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Tag correlation searches that create HIPAA candidate notables; (2) normalize UTC; (3) executive dashboard for legal discovery SLA.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `` `notable` `` (`_time`, `rule_name`, `status`), `index=dlp` `sourcetype=ms:o365:management` or `ms:o365:dlp` (`IncidentId`, `CreatedTime`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tag correlation searches that create HIPAA candidate notables; (2) normalize UTC; (3) executive dashboard for legal discovery SLA.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`notable` HIPAA_Breach_Candidate=1 earliest=-90d
| eval detect_time=_time
| join IncidentId type=left [
    search index=dlp sourcetype="o365:dlp" earliest=-90d
    | eval first_indicator=strptime(CreatedTime,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(first_indicator) as first_dlp by IncidentId ]
| eval hours_to_detect=round((detect_time-first_dlp)/3600,2)
| table IncidentId, first_dlp, detect_time, hours_to_detect, rule_name, status
| sort - hours_to_detect
```

Understanding this SPL

**Breach Discovery — Time-to-Detect from First PHI Indicator (§164.404)** — Measures elapsed time between earliest technical indicator of a potential PHI incident and formal incident creation so discovery timelines support Breach Notification Rule evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: `` `notable` `` (`_time`, `rule_name`, `status`), `index=dlp` `sourcetype=ms:o365:management` or `ms:o365:dlp` (`IncidentId`, `CreatedTime`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Invokes macro `notable` — in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **detect_time** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hours_to_detect** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Breach Discovery — Time-to-Detect from First PHI Indicator (§164.404)**): table IncidentId, first_dlp, detect_time, hours_to_detect, rule_name, status
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Histogram (hours_to_detect), Table (incidents), Single value (median detect hours).
visualization: Histogram (hours_to_detect), Table (incidents), Single value (median detect hours).
